name: mods-install
description: How to search for and install mods correctly, respecting loader compatibility and version isolation. Use when the user wants to add/install mods or asks for mod recommendations ("装模组","加mod","装mod","推荐模组").
body:
# Install mods

`search(action=mods, query, gameVersion, loader)` — real compatible builds + required
loader. Omit `source` to merge Modrinth+CurseForge results (don't guess IDs from memory).
Note the `loader` and `gameVersion` (and, if you need one exact build, the `version`)
of the result you pick — you'll pass them straight through to the install call below.

`instance(action=mods_install, id, source, instance, loader, gameVersion, version)` —
ALWAYS pass `instance` explicitly, even for "the selected one". Wrong/missing target
silently lands files in the wrong place: isolation ON puts mods under
`versions/<name>/mods/`; OFF shares `.minecraft/mods/`, leaking into every version.
ALSO always pass `loader` and `gameVersion` — the exact values `search` already
resolved for the mod you're installing. Omitting them makes the install silently fall
back to that mod's single most-recently-published file across EVERY loader and MC
version it ships, which is how a Fabric build ends up on a Forge/NeoForge instance, or
the wrong game version gets installed. Pass `version` too when you need one exact,
locked build rather than "newest compatible".

Install dependencies too, e.g. Fabric API for most Fabric mods — `search` surfaces them.
Verify with `instance(action=mods_list, instance)` or glob/read the `mods/` dir.

## Don't
- Don't put a Fabric mod on a Forge instance or vice versa (NeoForge/Quilt are distinct
  loaders too) — check each result's loader field first, THEN pass it as `loader` to
  `mods_install` (checking it isn't enough; the install call must carry it too).
- Don't skip dependencies — a missing one usually crashes on launch.
- Don't guess mod IDs/versions from memory — resolve via `search`.
